segments.s168-g153: چو + past verb (آمد) is a conventional poetic temporal clause meaning “when (he) came.” به before a place (ایوان) and before a person/name (گلشهر) is the preposition “to/into”; گفت is the reporting verb “said” with its subject understood (omitted pronoun) and introduces the following که-clause.
segments.s168-g154: که introduces the content of the speech (“that …”). این خوب رخ را is the definite object (را marks the object) meaning “this beautiful face.” بباید is a modal/obligative (”must/ought”) placed before the verb; نهفت here is the verb notion “be hidden” (an impersonal/obligative construction: “must be hidden”).
segments.s168-g155: تو is the vocative/second-person address “you.” بر پیش این نامور is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning “before/in the presence of this famed one” (بر + پیش together indicate location/in front of). زینهار is an admonitory interjection “beware/take heed” directed at the addressee.
segments.s168-g156: بباش is an imperative form (older prefix ب- + باش) meaning “be” or “remain” (second person). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” بدارش is imperative + clitic object (keep/hold her — دار as ‘hold/keep’ with -ش = ‘her’). پرستاروار is an adjectival adverb “like a guardian” describing the manner of keeping/attending.
segments.s168-g157: بر این نیز is a topical/time phrase “after this also / upon this as well.” بگذشت is the past verb “passed” (of time). یک چند روز is a duration expression “a few days” (یک + چند = several/a few days) placed after the verb.
segments.s168-g158: گران شد is a poetic predicate meaning “became heavy/affected” (here idiomatically “became sorrowful/anguished”); شد is the auxiliary/predicative “became.” فرنگیس گیتی فروز is the subject with an epithet (گیتی‌فروز = “world-illuminating”) following the proper name; Persian allows the predicate before the subject in such lines.
segments.s168-g73: بدو is the dative/benefactive 'to him/unto him' (here introducing the indirect object), followed by the name پیلسم; گفت is the main past-tense verb 'said'. بشتاب is an imperative 'hurry!' and زود is an adverb modifying it 'quickly/soon'. The order is 'to‑him Pilsam said: hurry quickly'.
segments.s168-g74: که is the subordinating conjunction 'for/because/that' giving the reason; دردی 'a pain' (indefinite) is the subject of the clause; the orthography has ب + دین for بدین 'to/into this' (here split in the data), then درد 'pain' repeated to mean 'this pain' or 'an additional pain', و links to سختی 'hardship', and فزود is the past form 'increased/grew'. The clause says this new pain increased the pain and hardship.
segments.s168-g75: فرنگیس is the proper name 'Farangīs'; را نیز is the object marker را plus نیز 'also' (here orthographically given as رانیز), خواهند is the auxiliary/future 'they will' and کشت is the verbal root meaning 'kill' (here object-oriented past/stem used with خواهند to mean 'will kill'). The clause states that they intend to kill Farangīs as well.
segments.s168-g76: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (do)'; هیچ‌گونه 'in no way/whatever' qualifies the prohibition; بر + ین here forms برین 'on/against this' (بر = against/toward, ین = this), کار 'action/deed' is the noun, and پشت in this construction means 'support/backing'. Together: 'Do not in any way be a backer/supporter of this deed.'
segments.s168-g77: به درگاه بردند is a past-tense verbal phrase 'they carried/took to the court' (به = to, درگاه = court/presence, بردند = they carried). مویش (مو + -ش) is 'her hair' and کشان is a participial/gerundial form 'dragging', so 'they took her to the court, dragging her hair.'
segments.s168-g78: بر here functions as 'before/upon' (introducing the people to whom she was shown); روزبانان 'the heralds/announcers', مردم 'the people', and again کشان 'dragging' — the clause describes that she was dragged before the people's heralds. The participial کشان modifies the action of carrying/dragging.
segments.s168-g79: جهانی here stands for 'the world' or 'all people' acting as subject; بدو is 'to him/unto him'; کرده is a past participle/auxiliary 'made/caused' (literally 'the world made to him'), and دیده پرآب 'his eye/eyes full of water' (دیده = eye, پرآب = filled with water/tearful). The sequence means that the whole world (i.e., everyone) looked to him and his eyes were brimming with tears.
segments.s168-g80: ز is the poetic preposition 'because of/from'; کردار 'deed/action' is the noun, بدگوهر is a compound adjective 'ill‑natured/evil‑charactered', and افراسیاب is the proper name Afrāsiyāb. The phrase gives the cause: 'because of Afrāsiyāb's wicked deeds.'
segments.s168-g129: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'. 'دیوانه' follows it as a simile/adverbial phrase ('like a madman'). 'از جای برخاستی' is a past-tense 2nd-person singular clause: 'you rose from the place/your seat' (برخاستی = you rose/stood up).
segments.s168-g130: 'چنین' = 'thus/so' linking this clause as the result of the previous action. 'خیره' is an adjective 'staring/astounded' modifying 'بد' ('the bad/evil one'); 'را' is the definite/object marker. 'بیاراستی' here is a past 2nd-person form meaning 'you caused/made/put (into that state)' — poetic usage. The whole means 'you thus made the evil one stare.'
segments.s168-g131: 'کنون' is the adverb 'now'. 'زو' is the poetic contraction 'ز' + 'او' = 'from him' (here written as separate tokens in the data). 'گذشتی' is past 2nd-person 'you passed/left' (you departed from him). 'به فرزند خویش' is the indirect-object phrase 'to/for your child' — note 'خویش' is split in the data as 'خوی' + 'ش' (possessive).
segments.s168-g132: 'رسیدی' is past 2nd-person 'you arrived/reached'. 'به' = 'to'. 'پیچاره' is a noun meaning 'the pitiable/poor one' (here the target of the action). 'پیوند خویش' = 'your bond/tie' (پیوند = bond/link; خویش = your/one's own). Together: 'you reached to attach your tie to the wretched one.'
segments.s168-g133: 'نجوید' is the negative verb form meaning 'did not seek/want' (classical/perfective negative). 'همانا' is an emphatic adverb 'truly/indeed' placed for emphasis. 'فرنگیس' is the subject (proper name). 'بخت' is the object 'fortune/fate' (what she did not seek).
segments.s168-g134: This is a series of coordinated negatives with 'نه ... نه ...' = 'neither ... nor ...'. 'اورنگ شاهی' is a compound meaning 'royal splendour/throne of kingship' (اورنگ = splendour/throne, شاهی = kingship). Then 'نه تاج و نه تخت' enumerates 'nor crown, nor throne.' The particles connect the list of things she did not seek.
segments.s168-g135: 'به فرزند' = 'for/to (your) son' (purpose/recipient). 'با کودکی' = 'with a child' (accompaniment or by means of a child). 'در نهان' = 'secretly' (locative/adverbial). These adverbial phrases set the circumstance/purpose for the following prohibition.
segments.s168-g136: 'درفشی' is 'standard/banner (bearing)' (a role or thing). 'مکن' is the negative imperative 'do not' (2nd person). 'خویشتن' is the object 'yourself' (emphatic). 'در جهان' = 'in the world' (locative). Literally: 'Do not make yourself a banner in the world.'
segments.s168-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning “when.” از + noun + دور گشت is a prefixed/compact way of saying “became distant / moved away from”; گشت is the past/intransitive sense “became/turned.” آفتاب is the subject placed after the verb for poetic word order: “when the sun became distant from the cypress.”
segments.s168-g2: سر شهریار is a possessive/genitive-like apposition (literally “the head of the king”), used metonymically for the king himself. اندرآمد here functions verbally (in older/poetic Persian) meaning “entered into / fell into,” followed by به خواب (“into sleep”). The clause reads “the king’s head entered into sleep” → “the king fell asleep.”
segments.s168-g3: چه خوابی is an exclamative idiom meaning “what a sleep / such a sleep.” که introduces the result clause: چَندین زمان برگذشت = “many/ several periods of time passed.” برگذشت is the past intransitive “passed by.” The whole segment gives the extent/duration of the sleep.
segments.s168-g4: نه ... و ... هرگز نگشت is negative parallelism: نه جنبید “he did not move/stir,” و بیدار هرگز نگشت “and he never became awake.” جنبید is simple past “moved,” بیدار is predicate adjective “awake,” and نگشت (negated by نه/هرگز) is the past/inchoative sense “became/turned (into).”
segments.s168-g5: چو again = “when.” از شاه شد is a compressed expression meaning “became from the king / when the king was gone”; شد is the intransitive/past “became.” گاه و میدان تهی = “time/occasion and arena/field (i.e., the court) became empty.” Word order is poetic; the verb (شد) precedes the complements.
segments.s168-g6: نه ... نه is a parallel negative: “neither … nor ….” با او means “with him” (伴/accompanying). سرو سهی is a poetic phrase for the tall/dark cypress; سهی is an epithet. The clause states that neither sun nor cypress remained with him.
segments.s168-g7: چپ و راست is a paired expression “left and right.” هر سو = “every side/direction.” بتابم is first-person singular present/subjunctive/poetic imperfect of تابیدن used here intransitively “I turn / I cast (my gaze),” and همی is a literary continuative particle meaning “still/indeed.” The line describes restless looking in all directions.
segments.s168-g8: سر و پای گیتی is a formulaic pair meaning “the head and foot (top and bottom) of the world.” نیابم is the negative present subjunctive/indicative form of یافتن (with negative prefix ن-): “I do not find / I cannot find.” همی repeats the literary continuative sense. The whole: “I cannot find any part (top or bottom) of the world.”
segments.s168-g17: ز introduces a source ('from'); سر ماهرویان is a genitive/grouping 'the heads of the moon‑faced (women)'. گسسته is a past participle placed before the noun کمند (poetic inversion) and describes the state of the ringlets: 'having been broken/torn'. The whole phrase reads 'from the heads of the moon‑faced ones (the) ringlets torn'.
segments.s168-g18: خراشیده is another past participle/adjective ('scratched'/'wounded') modifying روی ('face'); the conjunction و links that clause to بمانده نژند, where بمانده is a past‑state participle meaning 'remained' or 'was left' and نژند is an adjectival noun 'base/disgraceful'. Grammatically this gives two stacked descriptions: '(the) face scratched and disgrace remained.'
segments.s168-g19: همه بندگان is a subject group 'all the servants/attendants'. موی کردند باز is an idiomatic verb phrase: کردن + موی (to do/make hair) with باز meaning 'open/loose' here, so 'they let their hair down' or 'they opened their hair'. Past tense کردند places the action in the past.
segments.s168-g20: This is a nominal apposition naming the principal: فرنگیس (a woman's name) followed by modifiers مشکین (dark) and کمند دراز (long ringlets). Word order is noun + adjectives/modifiers (typical in poetry), giving 'Ferangis, (with) dark long ringlets.'
segments.s168-g21: برید is the past verb 'cut' (here used as a simple past; the provided lemma senses did not list 'cut', so the mapping below notes that). و is the coordinating 'and'. میان را uses را as the definite‑object marker ('the middle' as the object). به گیسو ببست: به introduces the instrument/means 'with/using a lock of hair', and ببست is the past of بستن here meaning 'bound' or 'tied'. The clause reads 'he cut and tied the middle with a lock of hair.'
segments.s168-g22: به introduces an instrument or accompaniment ('with/by'). فندق گل is a compound where فندق (lit. 'hazelnut') functions as a color/descriptor for گل ('flower' or ornament) — i.e. 'hazel‑colored flower' — and ارغوانرا is the object ('the purple/crimson [ornament]') with the object marker را omitted or folded into the poetic phrase. بخست (past of بستن) here again means 'fastened/tied' or 'secured'. Overall: 'with a hazel‑colored flower he fastened the purple ornament.'
segments.s168-g23: به آواز = 'with a voice/cry' (instrumental/attendant circumstance). بر جان افراسیاب uses بر + جان to mean 'against/upon the life of Afrasiab' or more idiomatically 'crying out for/against Afrasiab's life' — a poetic way to indicate the addressee/target of the cry. Word order is prepositional phrase + target name.
segments.s168-g24: همی is an early/poetic continuous/iterative marker meaning 'kept on/was continually'. کرد نفرین: کرد is simple past 'he did/uttered' and نفرین is 'a curse' — together 'he uttered a curse' (with همی implying repetition). و connects the clauses; می‌ریخت is an imperfective past form 'was pouring' (progressive past), and آب is its object 'water' (i.e. 'was pouring water'). The phrase thus describes ongoing cursing accompanied by pouring water.
segments.s168-g121: فریبنده دیوی is an adjective + noun phrase: فریبنده (an adjectival form meaning 'deceitful/enticing') modifies دیو(ی) 'demon'. ز دوزخ is a poetic prepositional phrase where ز = 'from' and دوزخ = 'Hell'. بجست is an archaic/past 3rd-person singular perfective verb meaning 'leapt' or 'sprang forth' and completes the clause.
segments.s168-g122: This clause names the affected person and his reaction: دل شاه ترکان is the nominal phrase 'the heart of the Turk king' (possessive relation expressed by juxtaposition in classical Persian). بیامد is a simple past 'came/occurred' used poetically before the subject, and بخست (past of بستن) means 'shut/closed' — together the verbs describe the heart being seized and closing. Note the inverted/poetic order where verbs can precede the noun phrase they relate to.
segments.s168-g123: بران is an adverbial/demonstrative 'upon that' or 'thereon' introducing the target of the following statement. اهرمن is the proper name Ahriman (the adversary); نیز = 'also/likewise'. نفرین 'curse' is the noun and سزد is an impersonal/classical present subjunctive-like predicate meaning 'it befits' or 'ought (to be)'. The construction expresses that it would be fitting for Ahriman also to pronounce a curse.
segments.s168-g124: که introduces a subordinate (result/that-) clause: پیچد is a present/subjunctive 3rd-person form 'may/shall turn'. روانت = روان + -ت is 'your soul/spirit' (possessive enclitic). سوی راه بد is a prepositional phrase 'toward the evil way' (سوی = toward, راه = path, بد = bad/evil). The clause gives the undesirable result expected.
segments.s168-g125: پشیمان شوی is a two-word predicate: پشیمان 'sorry/regretful' + شوی (2nd-person present/subjunctive/optative of شدن) 'you become' — together 'you will be sorry' or 'you will become regretful'. زین به‌روز دراز is a temporal/locative phrase: زین here functions like 'on/from this' (classical usage), به‌روز 'on the day' and دراز 'long' — 'on this long day'.
segments.s168-g126: بپیچی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive (or jussive) form of پیچیدن 'to turn/writhe' meaning 'you will turn/writhe'. زمانی 'for a while' gives duration. به گرم و گداز uses به + two nouns to indicate state: گرم 'hot/ardent' and گداز (poetic) 'burning, anguish' — together 'in burning torment'. The segment describes a temporary state of torment.
segments.s168-g127: ندانم is the negative present 1st-person 'I do not know'. که introduces the embedded question/clause. این گفتن بد is a nominalized clause: این 'this' + گفتن 'saying/story' + بد 'bad' — 'this bad saying'. ز کی است (written here as ز کی ست) means 'from whom is it' — ز = 'from', کی = 'who', ست = copula 'is'. The clause literally asks 'I do not know from whom this bad saying is.'
segments.s168-g128: و links the second interrogative. زین here functions classically as 'in/with regard to this' (archaic usage). آفریننده را رای چیست literally orders the words 'the creator (آفریننده) — ra (topic/object marker) — رای (counsel/plan) — چیست (what-is)'. رای چیست = 'what is the counsel/plan?' So the speaker asks also, 'and what is the Creator's counsel regarding this?'
segments.s168-g33: زنندش is the 3rd-person plural present verb زنند (“they strike/beat”) with the enclitic object -ش (“him”), so “they beat him.” همی is an adverbial particle marking a continuous or habitual action (here: “kept/continued to”). چوب is the instrument/noun “stick(s)” (here used without a preposition in older/poetic style). تا introduces a result/limit clause (“until/so that”), and تخم کین is a genitive/compound “seed (تخم) of hatred/vengeance (کین)” — the phrase is the object that will be effected in the following clause.
segments.s168-g34: بریزد is 3rd-person singular in the present/subjunctive form used after تا to express the result (‘may/was poured, spilled’). بر + ین here make a demonstrative/locative phrase (برین = “upon that/this”), with بوم توران زمین a stacked noun phrase meaning “the (native) soil/land of Turan.” Grammatically, بریزد takes تخم کین (from the previous segment) as its object and places it برین بوم توران زمین.
segments.s168-g35: نخواهم is first-person (present/future) negative of خواستن — “I do not want / I will not have.” ز is the poetic preposition “from,” so ز بیخ سیاوش = “from the root of Siavash” (genitive relation; the ezāfe is omitted in poetry). درخت is the noun “tree” (here a metaphor for lineage or offspring) and is the thing the speaker refuses to have.
segments.s168-g36: This is a standard negating list using repeated نه … و نه … meaning “neither … nor …” (anaphoric negation). Each نه negates the following noun: شاخ “branch/bough,” برگ “leaf,” تاج “crown,” تخت “throne.” The conjunction و simply links items; the overall effect is “no branch, no leaf, no crown, no throne.”
segments.s168-g37: همه is the universal determiner “all/every.” نامداران (literally “the famed/renowned ones”) is used as a plural noun — “the illustrious men.” آن انجمن is a demonstrative + noun “that assembly/meeting,” and together the phrase means “all the renowned men of that assembly.” Word order is adjective/noun grouping typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s168-g38: گرفتند is past tense 3rd-person plural of گرفتن “they took/they seized,” used here with نفرین as its object: “they took/raised (a) curse.” The particle/word برو in this line functions as an archaic locution meaning roughly ‘upon him’ (etymologically close to بر + او), and تن به تن is the idiom “person/body to person/body” meaning “one by one” or “each in turn.” The verb precedes the noun phrase (typical poetic inversion), so the full sense is “they took up a curse upon him, one by one.”
segments.s168-g39: که here introduces a clause (roughly “for/that/who”), connecting to the following question-like clause. از is the preposition “from/of/concerning.” شاه “king” and دستور “order/command” are coordinated by و. وز is best read here as the noun “weight/force” (وز) and links to لشکری (“of the army”), so دستور وز لشکری = “the command and the force/weight of the army.” The phrase lists powerful authorities presented as context for the judgment.
segments.s168-g40: ازین‌گونه is an adverbial phrase “in this way / of this kind.” نشیند is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of نشستن (‘to sit’) used here in a jussive/rhetorical sense — “would sit” (i.e. assume the seat of judgment). The verb-before-subject order (نش یند کس) is a poetic inversion: کس (“anyone/who”) is the subject and داوری (“judgment”) names the act: the line asks rhetorically who would sit in judgment like this given the authorities just listed.
segments.s168-g105: بیامد is a past tense verb 'he came' (subject omitted, third person). دمان here is an adverbial/time-word (archaic/poetic) meaning 'at that instant' or 'then' (this sense is not in the provided lemma senses). پیش + proper name is the usual Persian expression 'before/in the presence of X' (preposition + object). Word order: Verb + temporal/adverbial + prepositional phrase.
segments.s168-g106: دل is the semantic subject 'the heart'. از marks cause/reason ('from / because of'), so از درد خسته is a participial/adjectival phrase 'weary from pain' modifying دل. دو دیده is a numeral + noun 'two eyes' and پر آب is a postpositive adjectival phrase 'full of water' (i.e. tearful). Note classical Persian allows sequences like 'دل از درد خسته' (heart, exhausted from pain).
segments.s168-g107: بدو is an indirect-object form 'to him' (or 'unto him'); گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses list for گفت seems to show noun senses, so the verbal sense is mapped separately). شاها is the vocative 'O king!'. انوشه is a proper name. بدی here is ambiguous in the supplied senses (it can mean 'evil'), but in context it appears as part of the reported phrase after the vocative and may be part of a name or an archaic/poetic form — its lexical role is uncertain.
segments.s168-g108: روان را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker ('the soul' as object). به دیدار is a prepositional phrase 'for/to the meeting/encounter'. توشه is the noun 'provisions' and appears as the thing given. بدی at the end here functions in context like a past verb 'gave' (an archaic/poetic form) or is otherwise ambiguous in the supplied senses; syntactically the clause reads 'you/he gave the soul provisions for the meeting.'
segments.s168-g109: چه آمد is an interrogative phrase 'what came/happened' (چه = what; آمد = happened/befell). ز here is the poetic/genitive preposition 'from / because of' and بد 'bad/ill' gives the source 'from ill'. بر تو is 'upon you'; the vocative تو‌ای 'you, O...' is the familiar poetic address, and نیکخوی is an epithet 'good‑natured / kindly'.
segments.s168-g110: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that / which'. آوردت is the past-tense verb 'brought' with the second‑person enclitic ت 'to you' (i.e. 'brought you'). این روز is 'this day'; بد آرزوی is a compound adjectival phrase 'bad‑wished' (= ill‑omened). Word order: relative particle + verb(+enclitic) + demonstrative noun phrase + modifiers.
segments.s168-g111: چرا is the question word 'why'. بر دلت is a prepositional phrase 'upon your heart' (دل + enclitic 2nd-person ت = 'your heart'). چیره شد is a two-word verbal expression 'became dominant / gained the upper hand' (چیره = dominant/overcome; شد = became). رای دیو is a genitive-like sequence 'the counsel/plan of the demon' (رای = counsel/plan, followed by the possessor دیو).
segments.s168-g112: ببرد is a verb meaning 'takes / carries (away)' (here used as third-person singular form; classical Persian allows forms with initial ب-). از introduces the source/starting-point 'from' and رخت شرم is a collocation 'the appearance/garment of shame' (رخت = appearance/garment, شرم = shame). گیهان خدیو is a title phrase 'the world's khedive (ruler of the world)' — grammatically it names the agent/subject capable of the action 'ببرد'.
segments.s168-g145: بدو گفت = بدو (to him) + گفت (said): a common word order 'to him said' = 'he said to him'. زینسان که گفتی بساز is an embedded reported/imperative sequence: زینسان 'thus / in this way' + که (complementizer 'that'/'as') + گفتی (you said) + بساز (imperative 'make/arrange'). Together it introduces direct advice or a quoted command.
segments.s168-g146: مرا را کردی ... بی‌نیاز: مرا (م + را) is the accusative 'me' (م = me, را = object marker) and کردی is 2nd-person past 'you made/you did'. The phrase از خون او بی‌نیاز is a predicative complement: 'بی‌نیاز (free of need) از خون او (from his blood)', so the whole clause means 'you made me free from his blood.'
segments.s168-g147: سپهدار پیران is a titulary genitive: 'the commander (سپهدار) of the elders (پیران)'. بدان is the fused locative/adverbial 'ب + آن' meaning 'at that / because of that'. شاد شد is a compound predication 'became glad' (شاد = happy, شد = became/was).
segments.s168-g148: از اندیشه و درد آزاد شد is a coordinated complement: از introduces the things he is freed from ('اندیشه و درد' = thought/worry and pain/sorrow), آزاد is the adjectival predicate 'free', and شد is the past copula 'became/was'. The whole phrase means 'he was freed from thought and pain.'
segments.s168-g149: بیامد به درگاه = 'he came to the court' (بیامد past 'came', به درگاه 'to the court'). و او را ببرد coordinates another action: او را (him + object marker) + ببرد (here narrative past 'took/carried'), i.e. 'and took him.'
segments.s168-g150: بسی نیز بر روزبانان شمرد: بسی 'many/much' (intensifier), نیز 'also/moreover', بر + روزبانان + شمرد is a light-prepositional construction 'count(ed) ... among/over the heralds'. شمرد is the past 'counted/numbered' (here 'counted many among the heralds').
segments.s168-g151: بی‌آزار is an adverb/adjective meaning 'without harm / harmlessly'. بردش = برد + ش is the verb 'took/carried' with enclitic pronoun -ش 'him'. به سوی ختن means 'toward Khotan' (به سوی = toward; ختن here a place-name). The clause means 'he took him harmlessly toward Khotan.'
segments.s168-g152: خروشان همه درگه و انجمن is a verbless participial/adjunct clause describing the scene: خروشان 'roaring/tumultuous' modifies 'همه درگه و انجمن' ('the whole court and assembly'). Word order places the descriptive adjective before the nouns for vivid poetic effect.
segments.s168-g65: This is a short clause with the verb 'برید' (past) at the front — a common epic/past narrative order. 'برید' is the past action 'cut off'. 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that' modifying 'سر' (head). 'شاهوارش' is a compound adjective + enclitic pronoun: 'شاهوار' (lofty, regal) + -ش (his/its). 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from', followed by 'تن' (body) as the source. The object ('سر') appears immediately after the determiner, and the prepositional phrase 'ز تن' marks from what the head was severed.
segments.s168-g66: 'فگندش' (here a past, archaic/poetic form of فکندن ‘to throw’) is the verb meaning 'they threw it' or 'they cast it' and carries the object enclitic -ش 'it/his'. 'چو' is the simile marker 'like/as'. 'سرو سهی' is a descriptive phrase: 'سرو' (cypress) plus 'سهی' — a poetic epithet; together 'like a (poetic) cypress'. 'بر' is the preposition 'upon/on' and 'چمن' is the location 'meadow'. The clause therefore describes how/where the severed head was cast.
segments.s168-g67: 'همه شهر' is 'the whole city' (literally 'all the city'). 'پر' here is the adjective 'full (of)'; it takes the following nouns as the contents: 'زاری' (lamentation) and 'ناله' (moan/cry). The verb 'گشت' is the past 'became' (گشت = 'turned/was made'), so the clause literally: 'the whole city became full of lamentation and moaning.' Word order: topic — predicate — verb (typical classical Persian compactness).
segments.s168-g68: This is a locative/phenomenal clause describing the cityfolk's tears. 'به چشم اندرون' = 'in the inner eyes' (i.e. within people's eyes). 'آب' (water) is the subject that 'چون ژاله گشت' — 'turned/ became like dew'; 'چون' marks the simile 'like', 'ژاله' here is used as a tear/dew image. The verb 'گشت' again is 'became/turned into.' The whole clause says tears filled the inner eyes, being likened to dew.
segments.s168-g69: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. پیران is the subject 'the elders'. به گفتار بنهاد گوش is a poetic word order: بنهاد (past of نهادن 'to place/turn') takes گوش as its object, and به گفتار marks the direction/target ('toward the speech'); together بنهاد گوش به گفتار = 'lent an ear to the speech' (i.e. listened).
segments.s168-g70: ز تخت اندرافتاد is literal 'fell from the throne' with ز (poetic variant of از) marking source and اندرافتاد (past of درافتادن) meaning 'fell down' or 'fell off'. و زو رفت هوش contains زو = از او ('from him') and رفت هوش, an idiom where رفت (past of رفتن) = 'left/vanished' and هوش = 'awareness/consciousness' so رفت هوش = 'lost consciousness'.
segments.s168-g71: همی is the archaic/progressive particle marking continuous or habitual action. جامه را is the definite direct object ('the garment/robe') marked by را. بر + برش + کرد چاک: here بر (on/upon) and برش (literally 'a cut/slice') combine with کرد (past of کردن 'to make/do') and the noun چاک ('gash/tear') to mean 'made a gash in (his) garment' — the verb کردن takes چاک as the result ('made it a gash'). Word order is poetic but corresponds to 'he tore the robe, making a gash.'
segments.s168-g72: همی again marks ongoing action. کند is the verb 'does/makes' (here functioning as the simple action 'pulls/plucks' in context: he 'does' hair — i.e. pulls it). موی is the object 'hair/locks'. و links the next clause; همی ریخت خاک has ریخت (past of ریختن) used transitively/'to scatter/pour' with خاک ('dust') as its object — a conventional mourning gesture 'to cast dust' on oneself. Together: 'he tore/plucked his hair and scattered dust.'
segments.s168-g49: This phrase has numeral + noun + postpositive adjective: 'سه اسپ گرانمایه' = 'three prized steeds'. The verb 'کردند' is simple past, third-person plural, taking that noun phrase as its object ('they made/caused'). The final noun 'زین' (saddle) follows the verb and functions as an additional object/goal — here effectively 'they saddled (them)' or 'they provided saddles'.
segments.s168-g50: 'همی' is an emphatic/adverbial particle common in epic Persian (here: 'thereupon' / 'indeed'). 'برنوشتند' is past, 3rd pl. 'they wrote/inscribed' (بر‑ prefix + نوشتند). 'گفتی' is a deverbal noun meaning 'the saying/words/statement' (hence what was written), and 'زمین' is the lexical content of that saying — the word written. Word order is verb → noun-as-quotation, typical in older Persian.
segments.s168-g51: The preposition 'به' marks the destination/target: 'to/at the elders' (پیران). 'رسیدند' is past, 3rd pl. 'they arrived/reached.' 'هر سه سوار' is a numeral phrase meaning 'the three riders (all three)'; 'هر' here emphasizes the group of three as a unit.
segments.s168-g52: 'رخان' is an adjectival/participial word meaning 'loose, flowing' (describing manes or hair). 'پر ز' is a fixed poetic pattern: 'پر' + 'ز' = 'full of' (here 'پر ز خون' = 'full of blood'). 'همچو' introduces a simile 'like', and 'ابر بهار' = 'spring cloud' (the image of a reddened cloud). The sequence is descriptive, typical of epic imagery.
segments.s168-g53: 'برو' here functions as a discourse connective ('thereupon' / 'then') rather than a literal command. The verb complex 'بر شمردند' (بر‑ prefix + شمردند) means 'they recounted/related' or 'they enumerated'. 'یکسر سخن' means 'the whole speech' or 'the entire tale' (یکسر = entirely / as one piece), so the phrase says they recited the whole speech at once.
segments.s168-g54: The particle 'که' introduces the content clause ('that/what'). 'بخت' here means 'fate/destiny'. 'از بدیها' uses 'از' to mark cause or source ('because of/from evils'). 'چه' is interrogative/exclamatory 'what/how', and 'افگند' is past of افکندن 'to cast/throw'. 'بن' ('root/base/foundation') is the object of افگند; the word order is slightly inverted (poetic): 'what fate from evils cast down (as a foundation)'.
segments.s168-g55: 'یکی' here is indefinite 'a certain / a' introducing the event. 'زاریی' is a noun meaning 'a lament / wailing'. 'خاست' is past 3rd sg. 'arose / arose up'. 'کاندر' is an older fused locative ('ک + اندر') meaning 'in / within', so 'کاندر جهان' = 'in the world'.
segments.s168-g56: The verb 'نبیند' is an archaic negative/optative form related to 'نَ‑' + 'بیند', functioning here as 'saw not / has never seen'. With 'کسی' following it, the phrase means 'no one saw'. 'از کهان و مهان' enumerates groups ('from the ancients and the great/visitors'), i.e. 'none among the ancients or great ones.' The word order (verb before 'کسی') is typical in poetic inversion.
segments.s168-g89: This is a temporal + direction clause leading to the verb. بدو is an archaic prepositional/temporal marker here linking the duration to the arrival; روز و دو شب is a temporal phrase ‘two days and two nights’; بدرگه (به درگاه) is the goal 'to the court/threshold'; رسید is the past tense verb 'arrived/reached', placed at the end as typical SOV order in classical Persian.
segments.s168-g90: This is an object phrase followed by the verb دید. درنامور is a descriptive prepositional/adjectival element 'renowned/in fame' modifying the following noun; پرجفا is a prefixed adjective 'full of cruelty/ruthless' that directly modifies پیشه; پیشه here is a noun meaning 'practice/doing/sight' in a broad poetic sense; دید is the past-tense verb 'saw/beheld' completing the clause.
segments.s168-g91: فرنگیس را is the proper-name + definite-object marker را, marking Farangis as the direct object. دید is the past-tense verb 'saw'. چون introduces a simile/description 'as/like', and بیهشان is an archaic adjective/phrase meaning 'bereft (of sense)/stunned', describing her condition; the simile phrase follows the object and modifies it.
segments.s168-g92: This is a participial/clausal continuation describing her state. گرفته is a past participle 'seized/taken'; ورا is an older form of the short object pronoun (to her / upon her); روزبانان is the agent 'heralds/announcers'; کشان is a present-participial form 'dragging' that attaches to روزبانان to mean 'the heralds, dragging (her)'. Word order places the participle and the object before the agentive participial phrase.
segments.s168-g93: به introduces a locative/instrumental relation 'in/at/with'; چنگال is the noun 'claw/talon'; هر یک 'each one' (هر modifies یک); یکی تیغ تیز is a noun phrase 'one (each) a sharp sword' where یکی (one/a) precedes تیغ (sword) and تیز (sharp) follows as an adjective — typical poetic word order.
segments.s168-g94: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; درگاه is 'threshold/royal court' as the source; برخواسته is a compound past participle 'has risen/sprung up' used before the noun رستخیز (an uprising/resurrection) which names what has arisen. The overall order places the source first, then the participle+noun describing the event.
segments.s168-g95: همانگاه is an adverb 'immediately/at that moment' modifying the clause; پیران is the subject 'the elders' (plural); بیامد is the past-tense verb 'came'; چو introduces a simile 'like', and باد 'wind' completes the simile. Word order is adverb — subject — verb — simile.
segments.s168-g96: This clause is compact and somewhat idiomatic/telegraphic. کسی + کش forms a compound with کش as an agentive element ('one who .../bringer/driver'); خرد here functions as a nominal modifier 'of wisdom' (poetically 'a bringer/holder of wisdom'); بود is the past copula 'was/appeared'; گشتند is the plural past 'they became' and شاد is the predicate 'happy/joyful'. Grammatically: a compound noun phrase (کسی‌کش خرد) is predicated to exist (بود), followed by the result clause 'they became happy'.
segments.s168-g25: خروشش = خروش + -ش (third-person possessive clitic). The phrase places the possessed noun first ('his roar'), then the prepositional phrase 'به گوش سپهبد رسید' ('reached the spahbed's ear'). رسید is simple past (classical Persian past) meaning 'reached/arrived at'. Word order here is topical: possessed noun → indirect object ('to the ear of the spahbed') → verb.
segments.s168-g26: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). آن points back to the previously-mentioned sound; ناله و زار is a paired nominal phrase ('moan and wail') functioning as the object/modifier of نفرین ('curse') — i.e. 'that wailing curse'. شنید is past tense 'he heard'. The clause follows the typical Persian order: temporal marker → demonstrative + NP → verb.
segments.s168-g27: The clause shows inversion common in epic style: 'به گرسیوز' (to Garsivaz, indirect object) opens the clause, followed by the topical 'بدنشان' ('their body/persons' — here the theme/topic of what is spoken), then subject 'شاه' and verb 'گفت' ('said/told'). In modern prose one would expect 'شاه به گرسیوز گفت', but the order here foregrounds the addressee and topic.
segments.s168-g28: که introduces reported content ('that' / introducing an order). او را: او + object marker را (marks definite direct object). آورید is the 3rd-person plural present-subjunctive/jussive form used here to report a command ('bring' / 'let them bring'); in classical Persian the subjunctive often conveys reported orders. به کوی = 'into the quarter/street'; از نهفت = 'from hiding' (از + نهفت 'concealment').
segments.s168-g29: ز (poetic form of از) 'from' + پرده 'curtain/veil' gives 'from behind the curtain'; به درگه 'to the royal court'. بریدش is برید + ـش (object clitic) — here 'they tore him away' — and کشان is a verbal adjective/participle 'dragging' used adverbially to describe the manner. The sequence is largely directional: from X to Y, action verb with object clitic, then manner.
segments.s168-g30: بر before روزبانان مردم marks movement 'toward/to' the addressees; روزبانان مردم is a genitival grouping 'the people's heralds/announcers' (روزبانان modified by مردم). کشان again is the participial 'dragging', describing how they moved him — the participle follows the phrase it modifies, as is common in epic diction.
segments.s168-g31: بدان is an archaic/poetic particle (here formed from ب + دان) meaning roughly 'thereupon/with that' or 'to that end'. تا introduces purpose/result ('so that / in order that'). بگیرند is the 3rd-person plural subjunctive/jussive ('they should seize' / 'might seize') used after تا to mark intended action. موی سرش = 'the hair of his head' (موی 'hair' + سر 'head' + ـش possessive clitic). The object comes before the verb in this subordinate-purpose clause.
segments.s168-g32: بدرند is the subjunctive/jussive plural of دریدن 'to tear' (here 'they should tear off'). The repeated بر بر is a poetic placement of the preposition 'on/over/toward' (here functioning adverbially/emphatically with the following noun). همه qualifies چادرش: 'all of her veil/entire veil' (چادر + ـش possessive). As with the prior verbs, these subjunctives are part of the reported command — ordered actions to be carried out.
segments.s168-g137: که introduces a reported/relative clause ('that'). تا + زنده‌ای is a temporal clause 'as long as he lived' (تا = 'until/as long as', زنده‌ای = 'alive'); بر تو is the prepositional phrase 'upon you' placed before the noun نفرین ('curse') for poetic word order. بود is the past copula (here narrative/potential 'was/would be').
segments.s168-g138: پس از زندگی is a postpositional/time phrase meaning 'after life' (پس ... از = 'after'); دوزخ آیین بود is a nominal sentence with دوزخ ('hell') as subject and آیین بود ('was the rule/fate') as predicate — آیین here = 'custom/lot' and بود again is the past copula, giving the sense 'hell would be the lot/fate.'
segments.s168-g139: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). شاه is the subject; روشن کند is a verbal phrase where روشن ('bright/enlightened') with کند ('make/do' in subjunctive/optative form) yields 'were to enlighten/restore'; جان من ('my life') is the direct object following the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s168-g140: فرستد is the main verb of the apodosis ('(he) will send' or 'would send'), here as a prospective/potential form matching the conditional. ورا is the clitic object 'to him/it', followed by سوی ایوان من ('toward my iwan/hall') as the target/directional phrase. Word order places the verb before its object and the directional phrase after it, common in epic diction.
segments.s168-g141: گر is a conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic temporal particle meaning roughly 'now/at this moment'; اندیشه ('thought/care') is the noun and زین + کودک forms a genitive 'for/of this child' (zīn here is an older form meaning 'of/this' — an archaic usage). است is the copula 'is'. The clause means 'if now your mind/concern is for this child.'
segments.s168-g142: همانا is emphatic 'verily/truly'. که is a complementizer 'that'. این درد و رنج is the noun phrase 'this pain and suffering' and اندک است is the predicate 'is small/little' (اندک as adjective before the copula). The whole second clause gives an emphatic reassurance: 'truly, this pain and suffering is small.'
segments.s168-g143: بمان is an imperative/optative 'remain/stay'. تا introduces the temporal clause 'until' and گردَد (subjunctive of شدن) means 'become / be separated' (here 'become separated'); از کالبد is the prepositional phrase 'from the body'. Together: 'stay until he is separated from the body.'
segments.s168-g144: بپیش تو is a locative/prepositional phrase 'before you / in your presence'. آرم is a first‑person verbal form here ('I will place / I will set'), an archaic/poetic form; بدو is an indirect object 'to him'. ساز بد is a noun + adjective 'a bad/ill device or instrument' — ساز can mean 'instrument/means' and بد qualifies it. The clause therefore states the speaker's intention: 'I will set him before you and prepare/provide for him a poor/evil device.' Note the compact, poetic word order and some archaic verb forms.
segments.s168-g9: Structure: subject + verb + result-clause. "یکی" = "someone" (indefinite subject). "بد کند" is present/future/simple clause meaning "does evil/acts badly" (کند = does/makes). "نیک پیش آیدش" is a short clause meaning "good comes before/befalls him": نیک (good/auspicious) + پیش (before/in advance) + آیدش (آید + enclitic ش = "comes to him" or "befalls him"). The verb آید precedes the clitic ش which marks the indirect object (= to him).
segments.s168-g10: Parallel nominative/nominal phrases that state the fortunate result. "جهان بنده" = "the world (becomes) a servant" (literally "world servant") where the verb 'is/comes' is understood from context; "و بخت خویش آیدش" = "and his fortune comes to him": بخت (fortune) + خویش + ش (خویش is split in the word list into خوی + ش; together they mean "his own") + آیدش (comes to him). Word order places the possessed noun (بخت خویش) before the verb آیدش.
segments.s168-g11: "یکی" again = "some/one" as an indefinite subject. "جز به نیکی" = "except/by nothing but goodness" where جز = "except" and به نیکی = "by/with goodness" (به used adverbially: 'by/through'). "جهان نسپرد" = a negated past verb phrase: جهان (the world / this life) + نسپرد (past negative of سپردن, "did not entrust/hand over/reward"); together meaning "the world does not give/reward (him)" — the object is implied. The clause says that some who act only with goodness nevertheless are not rewarded by the world.
segments.s168-g12: "همی" is an invariant poetic marker meaning "continually/indeed" (here an adverb: 'constantly'). "از نژندی" = "from disgrace/ignominy" (از = 'from/because of', نژندی = 'disgrace, baseness'). "فرو پژمرد" = a compound verb where فرو intensifies the verb پژمرد (to wither/wilt) — literally "he/they utterly withered"; the subject (the person who received no reward) is carried over from the previous clause. The word-order places the adverb and source phrase before the verb, as typical in classical Persian.
segments.s168-g13: "مدار" here is an archaic/poetic imperative meaning roughly "do not hold / do not take" (negative command). "ایچ" = "not at all/none" (an emphatic negator). "تیمار" = "care/solicitude" (the direct object). "با او به هم" = "with him, together/at all" or "toward him" — با او = 'with him', and به هم emphasises the togetherness/reciprocity. The whole segment is an imperative telling the addressee to take no care toward that person.
segments.s168-g14: "به گیتی مکن" = "in the world do not (do)" where به گیتی = "in the world" (به marking location/sphere) and مکن is the negative imperative "do not". "جان و دل را" = "the soul and the heart" with را as the definite/object marker. "دژم" is an adjective/adverb meaning "sullen/grim" and follows the object it qualifies; the clause means "do not make your life and heart sullen in this world."
segments.s168-g15: "ز" is the poetic preposition "from." "خان سیاوش" refers to Siyâvash's court/house/title (here a source/location). "برآمد" is the past tense verb "arose/issued forth." "خروش" is the noun "a cry/uproar." The phrase sequence is agent/source (from X) → verb → result (a cry arose from X).
segments.s168-g16: "جهانی" = "worldwide/universal" qualifying the following event. "ز گرسیوز" = "from Garsivaz" (source). "آمد" = "came/happened." "به جوش" is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning "in a surge/with boiling intensity" (به marking manner and جوش = 'surge/boiling/ardor'). The clause says a universal tumult came forth from Garsivaz in a surge of passion/noise.
segments.s168-g113: بکشتی is a 2nd-person singular past tense verb ('you killed') in poetic/classical Persian (the b- prefix appears often in older forms). سیاووش is the definite object; را is the accusative/object marker attaching to the proper name. بی‌گناه is a postpositive adjective ('innocent') modifying سیاووش.
segments.s168-g114: به خاک اندر انداختی is a verbal clause: به ... اندر (towards/into ... within) + انداختی (you threw/cast) — together 'you cast into the earth/dust.' نام و جاه is the compound direct object 'name and rank' (no را here because of poetic omission/inversion). Word order places the goal phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s168-g115: به ایران رسد uses رسد (present/optative 'reach' or 'may reach') with به + place 'to Iran'. زین بدی آگهی is a genitival phrase 'the news (آگهی) of this evil' — here زین is the fused/poetic form meaning 'of this' (not the 'saddle' sense; that sense is not in the provided list).
segments.s168-g116: که شد خشک پالیز سرو سهی: که introduces a causal/result clause ('for/because'), شد is 'became/has become', and خشک is the resulting adjective 'dry/withered'. پالیز سرو سهی is a noun phrase 'the orchard's (pālīz) cypress (saru) — سهی is a poetical epithet ('dark/black') modifying سرو.
segments.s168-g117: بسا is a quantifier meaning 'many' (or 'often'); تاجداران ایران‌زمین is a compound noun phrase 'the crown-bearers / sovereigns of the land of Iran' (تاجداران = rulers). The quantifier modifies the whole noun phrase.
segments.s168-g118: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'tājdarān' ('who'), با لشکر آیند is 'they come/arrive with an army' — آیند is 3rd-person plural present/optative 'they will come' (used prophetically). پردرد و کین are descriptive: 'full of sorrow (pained) and of vengeance/hatred' describing the coming host or its mood.
segments.s168-g119: جهان آرمیده is 'the world lies at rest / is subdued.' ز دست بدی is an ablative/agent phrase 'by the hand/power of evil' using the poetic preposition ز ('from/by'). Here دست functions as 'power/hand' (instrument/agent).
segments.s168-g120: شده آشکارا ره ایزدی: شده ('has become') plus آشکارا ('clear/openly') modifies the noun phrase ره ایزدی ('the divine way/path'), so the clause means 'the divine path has become manifest/clear.' The past participle/auxiliary ordering is typical of classical poetic phrasing.
segments.s168-g57: سیاووش را: 'سیاووش' is the direct object and the enclitic را marks it as definite/specific. 'دست‌بسته' is a compound adjectival phrase (literally 'hand-bound') postposed to the noun phrase, describing his condition. 'چو سنگ' is a simile ('چو' = 'like/as') modifying 'دست‌بسته' (i.e., bound as if a stone). Word order is typical Persian: object phrase before the verb.
segments.s168-g58: فگندند is an archaic past plural verb meaning 'they threw / they cast' (older equivalent of انداختند). 'در' is the preposition here meaning 'in/on/around' (governing گردنش). گردن‌ش is 'neck' with the enclitic possessive -ش 'his'. پالهنگ is the noun placed after the prepositional phrase and is the object of the throwing; in context it denotes a leopard or leopard-skin. The verb applies to the whole preceding object phrase.
segments.s168-g59: به دشتش کشیدند: 'به' here marks direction ('to/toward') and plus 'دشتش' ('his/that plain') gives 'they pulled/dragged (him) to the plain'. کشیدند is past plural 'they dragged/drew'. 'پر آب روی' is an adjectival phrase: پرِ آب = 'full of water' modifying روی 'face' (i.e. his face streaming/filled with water). The sequence places the directional clause first and the descriptive phrase immediately after.
segments.s168-g60: پیاده دوان: two adverbial words meaning 'on foot' (پیاده) and 'running/rushing' (دوان) describing how they moved. در به پیش گروی: 'در' here functions as a local/directional preposition ('into/in'), 'به' again marks direction ('to/toward'), and 'پیش' means 'before/in the presence of' (i.e. toward somebody or something). گروی is a noun in this slot (likely a name or group), but its senses listed in the dataset don't match the usage, so its precise semantic label is uncertain; syntactically it is the goal of the motion.
segments.s168-g61: تن پیل وارش: 'تن' = 'body'. 'پیل' here is used figuratively to indicate great size (elephant/huge). وارش is a bound/poetic element (in the provided senses it appears as 'rainfall' but here functions as part of a compound describing the body) — its exact lexical sense is archaic/poetic and does not behave like a standalone modern word. بران = 'upon that/onto it' (directional/adverbial), and 'گرم خاک' = 'the warm earth' (گرم modifies خاک). segments.s168-g62: فگندند is the same archaic past plural 'they threw/they cast'. The conjunction و connects to the following clause. از کس نکردند باک is an idiomatic negative construction: literally 'they did not (نکردند) [have] fear (باک) of/from anyone (از کس)'; in natural English, 'they feared no one.' Note that نکردند here is the negated past of کردن used with باک to mean 'to have fear', so the surface senses of کردن in the list are being used idiomatically.
segments.s168-g63: یکی تشت بنهاد: 'یکی' = 'a certain / someone'. 'تشت' = 'a large basin'. بنهاد is an archaic past form (بنهاد = 'placed/put down, set') used in narrative. 'گروی' again appears as the noun in this position (likely a name or group), but the senses provided for گروی do not match; syntactically it is the location/recipient ('before Goruy' or 'before the group').
segments.s168-g64: بپیچید is an older/past narrative form of پیچیدن with a prefixed ب-; here it means 'they turned / they twisted (him)'. چون = 'like/as', introducing a simile. گوسفندانش = 'his sheep' (گوسفندان + possessive -ش), and روی here = 'face' or 'in front' (so the simile is 'they turned him as one would turn the faces of his sheep' — a poetic comparison). segments.s168-g41: بیامد is the past-tense verb 'came' (classical form) placed before the following nominal phrase for poetic effect. "پر از خون" is a postpositive adjective phrase 'full of blood' that modifies "دو رخ" ('two cheeks'); "پیلسم" is a name/epithet attached after the noun (classical apposition/possessive ordering), so the whole phrase reads 'his two cheeks, full of blood.'
segments.s168-g42: This is two parallel predicative phrases: "روان پر ز داغ" = 'his spirit/soul full of dāgh (anguish/grief)' and "رخان پر ز نم" = 'his cheeks full of moisture/tears.' "پر ز" is the classical 'full of' construction (= پر از). The conjunction و links the two states.
segments.s168-g43: به نزدیک + name is a classical prepositional phrase meaning 'to/at the presence of' or 'toward' someone; here it marks the recipients ('to Lahak and Farshidvord'). The conjunction و simply links the two names.
segments.s168-g44: سراسر functions as an adverb/adjective 'throughout/entire' modifying سخنها ('the speeches/words'); همه adds emphasis 'all.' یاد کرد is a light-verbal construction where یاد (remembrance/mention) plus کرد ('did/made') means 'recalled/related' — 'he recounted all the words.'
segments.s168-g45: که introduces a subordinate statement 'that ...'. The phrase "دوزخ به از بوم افراسیاب" uses the classical comparative pattern "به از X" meaning 'better than X' or 'preferable to X'; thus 'hell (دوزخ) is better than the homeland of Afrasiyab.'
segments.s168-g46: نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'one must not/there should not be.' The cluster ب + دین = بدین is the classical/deictic 'in/at this' (prefix بِ- + demonstrative), so بدین کشور = 'in/for this country.' آرام و خواب ('peace and sleep') are the two nouns conjoined, giving the sense 'there should be no peace or sleep in this country.'
segments.s168-g47: بتازیم is the first-person-plural jussive/imperative-like form 'let us rush/attack' from تاختن; it expresses a hortative command. نزدیک پیران شویم is 'let us come near/approach the elders' where شویم is the first-person-plural subjunctive/optative 'become/come to be' (here 'let us be/let us become ...').
segments.s168-g48: به introduces the purpose/relation 'to' or 'for'; تیمار و درد ('care and sorrow') are two coordinated nouns describing the activity or condition, and اسیران is a genitival/associative noun 'of the captives.' شویم repeats the hortative/subjunctive 'let us be' or 'let us become' — together: 'let us be to the captives as care and sorrow' i.e. 'let us attend to / share the captives' suffering.'
segments.s168-g97: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” چشم گرامی is a noun phrase with the adjective following the noun (چشم = “eye,” گرامی = “noble/valued”). به + رسید uses رسید (past) intransitively with به to indicate the target of the gaze: “(the) eye reached/fell upon Piran.” Word order is typical poetic Persian: temporal marker + subject phrase + verb.
segments.s168-g98: شد is the perfective/copular verb “became/was” and here introduces the result clause. از خون is a causal phrase “because of/from the blood.” دیده functions as ‘sight/eye’ (the thing seen or the ability to see) and رخش is “countenance/face” (not the horse here), so the phrase means literally “from the blood the sight of his face became vanished” = “his face disappeared from view because of the blood.” ناپدید is the predicate adjective “vanished/gone.”
segments.s168-g99: بدو (to him) marks the indirect object (“he said to him”). گفت here is the past-tense verb “said” (the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense, so sense is marked -1). با من means “to/with me” indicating the offended party. چه بد ساختی is an exclamatory/interrogative phrase: چه = “what,” بد = “bad/evil,” ساختی = 2nd-person past “you did/made,” so together “What bad have you done (to me)?”
segments.s168-g100: چرا is the interrogative “why.” خیره is an adjective/adverb meaning “stunned/dazed/astonished” describing the state in which someone was thrown. بر + آتش is the prepositional phrase “onto the fire,” and انداختی is the 2nd-person past of انداختن “you threw/cast.” The subject is omitted and supplied by the verb ending (you).
segments.s168-g101: ز اسپ is a poetic prepositional phrase “from (his) horse.” اندر افتاد is a compound verb meaning “fell (down/into).” پیران is the subject (the proper name Piran) and follows the verb in this poetic word order. به خاک is the directional phrase “to/onto the ground.”
segments.s168-g102: همه جامه = “all (his) garments.” پهلوی here marks location “at the side/flank.” کرده is the perfect participle “having made/caused” (resultative), and چاک is the noun/adjective “a tear/gash” (so کرده چاک = “had made [them] torn” or “had rent them”). segments.s168-g103: بفرمود is an honorific past verb “he commanded/said.” تا introduces a subordinate purpose/result clause here (so that/in order that). روزبانان is the subject of the subordinate clause (“the heralds”), and در begins the locative phrase (“inside”).
segments.s168-g104: زمانی = “for a while” (adverbial). ز (poetic از) + فرمان = “from/away from the command.” بتابند is 3rd-plural present-subjunctive of تابیدن in the sense “turn/bend,” used because تا introduced a subjunctive purpose (“may turn”). سر (head) is the direct object/part being turned: “turn their heads away from the order.” Word order places the adverb and prepositional phrase before the verb, typical in poetry.
segments.s168-g81: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause here ('that'). این هول کاریست is a nominal phrase meaning 'this terror is a thing/matter' where کاریست is the archaic/poetic 3rd-person copula/nominalizer ('is a matter'). بادرد و بیم is a prepositional/adverbial phrase (با + درد و بیم) meaning 'with pain and fear' modifying کاریست.
segments.s168-g82: که (again) continues the subordinate/explanatory clause ('that'). اکنون is temporal 'now'; فرنگیس را is the definite object marked by را (the person affected); بر دو نیم is a prepositional phrase 'into/in two halves' where the verb is deliberately omitted here and supplied by the next line (ellipsis across the line break).
segments.s168-g83: زنند is the 3rd-person-plural verbal form of زدن used here to mean 'they strike/cut' (completing the object phrase from the previous line: 'فرنگیس را بر دو نیم زنند'). The enclitic -ند is part of the verb; و links the two clauses; شود is the 3rd-person-singular of شدن 'become' and introduces the result clause 'the kingship becomes ruined' (پادشاهی تباه).
segments.s168-g84: This clause states the consequence/result. مر is an archaic/poetic particle here (listed as 'me/to me' in the lexicon) used idiomatically in the line; او را is the definite object 'him'/'her' (context supplies referent), نخواند is the negative 3rd-person form of خواندن 'to call/ name' meaning 'will call/name', and کسی نخواند (کسی here in the sense 'no one') yields 'no one will call (him)'. نیز شاه places 'king' as the name/title that will not be applied ('will not call him king either').
segments.s168-g85: ز آخر is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the end' = 'at last/ finally'. بیاورد is the 3rd-person verb 'brings/ brought/ brought forth' (in epic narrative forms like this the present form often narrates immediate action), and پس is an adverb 'then/thereupon'. پهلوان is the noun 'champion/hero' and here functions as the subject introduced after the verb (poetic inversion).
segments.s168-g86: This is a compact nominal phrase describing the champion: ده اسپ = 'ten horses/steeds' (a possession/scale of his force); سوار functions as 'rider/horseman' (here qualifying that he is mounted/a horseman); آزموده is a past-participle adjective 'proven/experienced'; جوان is used as 'young (man)'. The whole phrase modifies or identifies the پهلوان just mentioned.
segments.s168-g87: This is a coordinated list of agents: خود 'he himself' (emphatic), و 'and', گرد رویین here is a compound proper name (Gerd‑Ruyin) rather than the ordinary words, then و 'and' and فرشیدورد (a personal name). The sequence enumerates who joined the action in the next segment.
segments.s168-g88: برآورد is a narrative verb 'brought up/raised/put forth' (here: assembled or sent up); زان 'from that' + راه 'way/road' gives the source/direction 'from that road'; ناگاه is an adverb 'suddenly'; گرد in this clause functions as a noun 'troop/host' (object of برآورد). The word order (verb before source and adverb) is typical of epic/poetic inversion.
